title: Career Changers From Another Industry to Solicitor
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Switching careers into law is more common than you think and more achievable. This episode hears from people who came from finance, healthcare, tech, and other fields before making the move to the Law Society. We cover what the transition actually involves, how prior experience can be an asset, and the practical steps to get yourself on track for the FE-1.

title: Cover Letters for Training Contract Applications What Works
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: A strong cover letter can be the difference between an interview and a rejection. This episode breaks down what Irish law firms are actually looking for, the common mistakes that sink otherwise good applications, and how to write a cover letter that gets you in the room. Includes real examples of what works and what doesn't.

title: Getting a Training Contract Outside Dublin Real Stories
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: The training contract market isn't just a Dublin story. This episode speaks to candidates who secured contracts in Cork, Galway, Limerick, and beyond what those firms look for, how the regional market differs, and why going outside the capital might actually give you an edge. Real stories, honest advice.

title: How I Finally Got My Training Contract Five Candid Stories
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Five candidates. Five journeys. All of them eventually got there. This episode collects candid accounts of what it actually took the rejections, the pivots, the lucky breaks, and the lessons learned. If you're in the middle of the hunt, this one is for you.

title: How to Ace a Law Firm Interview
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Law firm interviews follow patterns and once you know them, you can prepare. This episode walks through the most common interview formats used by Irish firms, the questions you should expect, how to talk about your FE-1 performance confidently, and the soft skills that separate candidates who get offers from those who don't.

title: How to Write a CV That Law Firms Actually Read
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Most legal CVs look the same which means most get skipped. This episode covers how to structure a CV for Irish law firm applications, what to include and what to cut, how to present your FE-1 status, and how to make your experience stand out even if you don't have a first-class degree or blue-chip internship.

title: In-Office Training What Your Two Years as a Trainee Actually Look Like
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: The training contract is two years of structured, supervised practice but what does that actually mean day to day? This episode gives an honest account of life as a trainee solicitor in Ireland: seat rotations, billable hours, the learning curve, the relationships you build, and how to make the most of it before you qualify.

title: Internships, Networking and the Side Door into Law Firms
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Not everyone walks in through the front door. This episode explores how internships, work placements, and strategic networking can get you noticed by firms before the formal application process begins. We cover how to approach firms, what to say, and how to turn a week's placement into a training contract offer.

title: Rejection, Resilience and the Training Contract Hunt
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Rejection is part of the process but it doesn't have to define it. This episode is a frank conversation about the emotional reality of the training contract hunt: the silence after applications, the near-misses, and how candidates kept going. Practical strategies for staying motivated and learning from every round.

title: Smaller Firms, General Practice, and Why They Might Be the Better Choice
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Big firm isn't the only option and for many candidates, it isn't the right one. This episode makes the case for smaller firms and general practice: broader exposure, faster responsibility, stronger client relationships, and a path to qualification that suits a different kind of ambition. Honest insight from solicitors who chose this route and never looked back.

title: Studying the FE-1 While Working Full Time
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Most FE-1 candidates aren't studying full time they're working, managing families, and fitting exam prep around everything else. This episode is built for them. We cover realistic study schedules, the subjects that need the most time, how to use audio content on the go, and the mindset shifts that make part-time study sustainable.

title: The International Route to Irish Law
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Ireland attracts candidates from across the world and the route for international applicants has its own distinct steps. This episode explains the recognition process for overseas qualifications, how to navigate the Law Society's requirements if you qualified abroad, and what international candidates need to know about the FE-1 and the training contract market in Ireland.

title: The Mature Student's Route into Law
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Coming to law later in life brings its own pressures and its own advantages. This episode speaks directly to mature students: how to approach the FE-1 with life experience on your side, how firms view older applicants, and how to position your background as a strength rather than an explanation.

title: The Milk Round How to Handle Big Firm Applications
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: The annual law firm recruitment round is competitive, fast-moving, and unforgiving if you're unprepared. This episode explains how the milk round works, when to apply, what large Irish firms are looking for, how to manage multiple applications at once, and what to do if your first round doesn't go to plan.

title: The Non-Law Graduate Who Made It to the Law Society
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: You don't need a law degree to become a solicitor in Ireland. This episode follows the path of non-law graduates what the conversion route looks like, how the FE-1 fits in, and what it's like to sit exams alongside law graduates. A practical and encouraging listen for anyone coming to law from a different discipline.

title: The PPC Explained What Happens After You Pass the FE-1
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: Passing the FE-1 is a milestone, not the finish line. This episode explains the Professional Practice Course (PPC) what it covers, how it's structured, what to expect during indentures, and how the final stages of qualification work. Everything you need to know about the road from FE-1 pass to qualified solicitor.

title: What is the FE-1 Everything You Need to Know
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: New to the FE-1? Start here. This episode gives you the full picture what the examination is, which subjects are tested, how it's structured, when it runs, and what standard is expected. The essential orientation episode for every candidate at the beginning of their FE-1 journey.

title: Your 5-Step Roadmap to Becoming a Solicitor in Ireland
subject: Bonus
episodeNotes: From deciding to pursue law to the day you're admitted as a solicitor this episode maps out every stage of the journey. Five clear steps, explained plainly: the FE-1, the training contract, the PPC, indentures, and admission. The big-picture episode that shows you where you're going and what it takes to get there.

title: Types of Resolutions
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Companies make formal decisions by passing resolutions, but not all resolutions are the same. This episode distinguishes ordinary, special, and written resolutions under the Companies Act 2014, the voting thresholds required for each, and when each type must be used.

title: General Meetings - AGMs and EGMs
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: The AGM and EGM are the formal mechanisms through which members exercise control over a company. We cover the requirements for convening each type of meeting, notice periods, quorum, voting rights, and the consequences of procedural defects. A practical topic for FE-1 corporate governance questions.

title: Director Meetings and Board Governance
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: How do directors make decisions, and what happens when proper board procedure is not followed? This episode covers board meetings, quorum, minutes, written resolutions of directors, and the legal effect of decisions made improperly. Governance procedure is tested more often than candidates expect.

title: Examinership
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Examinership is Ireland's rescue procedure for insolvent but viable companies. This episode explains the process from petition to confirmation of a scheme of arrangement, the role of the examiner, and the protection from creditors during the process. A major topic for FE-1 corporate insolvency questions.

title: Receivership
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: A receiver is appointed by a secured creditor to realise security and recover a debt. We cover the appointment, powers, and duties of a receiver, the distinction between fixed and floating charge receivers, and the receiver's liability to the company and third parties. Often tested alongside charges.

title: Liquidation and Winding Up
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Winding up brings a company's life to an end. This episode covers voluntary and compulsory liquidation, the grounds for a winding up order, the appointment and role of the liquidator, and the order of priority for distributing assets. A comprehensive topic and a reliable FE-1 question area.

title: Voidable Transactions in Corporate Insolvency
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: When a company is wound up, certain pre-insolvency transactions can be set aside. This episode covers fraudulent preferences, reckless trading, related party transactions, and the liquidator's power to unwind them under the Companies Act 2014. Critical for FE-1 questions involving creditor protection on insolvency.

title: The Corporate Enforcement Authority
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: The Corporate Enforcement Authority (CEA) replaced the ODCE and is responsible for investigating and prosecuting breaches of company law in Ireland. This episode covers its establishment, functions, investigative powers, and interaction with the Director of Public Prosecutions.

title: What is Company Law? The Nature of a Company
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: This episode opens Company Law by examining what a company actually is and why it exists as a distinct legal entity. We look at the foundational nature of incorporation under the Companies Act 2014 and the consequences of legal personality. Essential groundwork for every other Company Law topic on the FE-1.

title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Incorporation
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: We weigh the practical and legal benefits of incorporation limited liability, perpetual succession, transferable shares against the regulatory burdens that come with it. Understanding this balance is a common starting point for FE-1 problem questions on company formation.

title: Section 31 and the Statutory Contract
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 31 of the Companies Act 2014 creates a statutory contract between the company and its members. This episode explains what that means in practice, who can enforce it, and the limits of enforcement. A frequently tested concept in FE-1 exam questions on member rights.

title: LTDs versus DACs - Key Differences
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Not all private companies are the same. This episode draws out the key distinctions between a Limited Company (LTD) and a Designated Activity Company (DAC) under the Companies Act 2014. Knowing which company type applies to a given scenario is essential for exam accuracy.

title: The Salomon Principle
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Salomon v Salomon [1897] is the cornerstone of company law and this episode gives it the full treatment it deserves. We cover the separate legal personality rule, its origins, and its significance for creditors, shareholders, and directors. Expect this case to appear directly or indirectly in almost every FE-1 Company Law question.

title: Disregarding or Lifting the Corporate Veil
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Sometimes courts will look behind the corporate structure and hold individuals personally liable this is lifting the veil. We examine the statutory and judicial grounds for doing so, including fraud, agency, and the relevant Irish and UK case law. A core exam topic with a consistent question pattern.

title: Section 158 - Delegation of Management Powers
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 158 of the Companies Act 2014 vests management of the company in the directors. This episode examines how that delegation works, what powers are reserved to members, and the constitutional balance within a company. Critical for questions on director authority and internal decision-making.

title: Corporate Capacity and the Indoor Management Rule
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Can a company be bound by a contract entered into without proper authority? This episode covers the Indoor Management Rule (the rule in Royal British Bank v Turquand), its statutory expression in the Companies Act 2014, and the protection it affords to third parties dealing with companies in good faith.

title: Fixed and Floating Charges - Definitions and Priority
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Companies raise finance by granting charges over their assets. This episode explains the distinction between fixed and floating charges, the registration requirements under the Companies Act 2014, and how priority between competing charges is determined. Heavily tested in FE-1 insolvency and corporate finance questions.

title: Fixed vs Floating Charges and Crystallisation
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Building on the last episode, we dig into when a floating charge crystallises and becomes fixed, the consequences for the company and creditors, and the key Irish case law on characterising a charge. Exam questions often test whether candidates can correctly categorise a charge in a given set of facts.

title: Retention of Title Clauses
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: A retention of title clause allows a seller to retain ownership of goods until paid in full. We examine how these clauses operate under Irish law, the conditions for their validity, and how they interact with floating charges and insolvency proceedings. A practical topic that appears regularly in FE-1 commercial law scenarios.

title: Types of Directors and the Duty to Act in Good Faith
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Not all directors are equal in law. This episode distinguishes executive, non-executive, shadow, and de facto directors, and explains the core fiduciary duty to act in good faith in the interests of the company under Section 228(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014. The starting point for all director duty questions.

title: Conflict of Interest and the Duty of Skill, Care and Diligence
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Directors must avoid conflicts between their personal interests and their duties to the company, and must act with the care and skill of a reasonably diligent person. This episode works through both duties under the Companies Act 2014 with supporting Irish case law and practical exam application.

title: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: What happens when a director breaches their fiduciary duties? We cover the full range of remedies available to the company account of profits, injunction, rescission, and personal liability and how courts have applied them in Irish and UK cases. Essential for any FE-1 question asking you to advise on director misconduct.

title: Section 238 and Substantial Non-Cash Asset Transactions
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 238 of the Companies Act 2014 restricts directors from acquiring or disposing of substantial non-cash assets with the company without member approval. This episode explains the thresholds, the approval process, and the consequences of non-compliance. A technical but regularly tested statutory provision.

title: Section 239 and Loans to Directors
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 239 restricts companies from making loans to directors without proper authorisation. We cover the scope of the prohibition, the exceptions, and the civil and criminal consequences of breach. Often paired with conflict of interest questions on the FE-1.

title: Section 212 - Oppression and Disregard of Members
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 protects minority shareholders from oppressive conduct by the majority or the company itself. This episode examines the statutory remedy, the case law defining oppression, and how Irish courts have applied the provision. A high-value topic for FE-1 minority shareholder questions.

title: Foss v Harbottle and the Derivative Action
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: The rule in Foss v Harbottle (1843) prevents individual shareholders from suing on behalf of the company but there are exceptions. This episode covers the rule, the fraud on the minority exception, and the statutory derivative action under the Companies Act 2014. A complex but examinable topic.

title: Restriction of Directors - Section 819
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 provides for the restriction of directors of insolvent companies from acting as directors for five years. We examine the grounds, the process, the role of the Corporate Enforcement Authority, and the defences available. Appears frequently in FE-1 insolvency scenarios.

title: Disqualification of Directors - Section 839
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Disqualification goes further than restriction and can result in a complete ban from acting as a company director. This episode covers the grounds for disqualification under Section 839, both automatic and court-ordered, and the consequences of acting while disqualified.

title: Dividends and Distributions
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: A company can only pay dividends out of distributable profits but what counts as distributable? This episode explains the rules on distributions under the Companies Act 2014, the consequences of unlawful dividends, and director liability for authorising improper payments. A deceptively technical topic.

title: Transfer of Shares
subject: Company Law
episodeNotes: Shares in a private company are not freely transferable the articles typically restrict transfer. This episode covers the mechanics of share transfer, pre-emption rights, the role of the board in refusing a transfer, and the legal consequences of improper refusal.

title: History and Origins of Bunreacht na hEireann
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Ireland's 1937 Constitution did not emerge in a vacuum it was shaped by the Free State Constitution of 1922, the political context of the time, and the influence of Catholic social teaching. This episode traces the historical and ideological origins of Bunreacht na hEireann and explains why that history still matters for constitutional interpretation today.

title: Constitutional Interpretation
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: How do Irish courts read the Constitution? This episode examines the key approaches literal, purposive, historical, and natural law and the case law that illustrates when each applies. Understanding interpretive methodology is essential for structuring any FE-1 constitutional law answer.

title: Amendment of the Constitution - Articles 46 and 47
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The Constitution can only be amended by referendum under Articles 46 and 47. We examine the procedure for proposing and approving amendments, the role of the Oireachtas and the people, and the significance of key constitutional referendums in Ireland's legal history.

title: The Doctrine of Separation of Powers
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The separation of powers divides governmental authority between the legislature, executive, and judiciary. This episode examines the constitutional basis for that division in Ireland, the leading cases on encroachment between branches, and why the doctrine matters for the validity of legislation.

title: The Oireachtas and Legislative Power - Article 15
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 15 vests the exclusive power to make laws in the Oireachtas. We examine what that exclusivity means, the consequences of sub-delegation, and the cases in which legislation has been struck down for impermissibly delegating legislative power. A key topic for separation of powers questions.

title: The Government and Executive Power - Article 28
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 28 vests executive power in the Government. This episode covers the collective responsibility of Cabinet, the constitutional conventions governing government formation, and the cases that have tested the boundaries of executive power in Ireland.

title: The Courts and Judicial Power - Articles 34 to 37
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Articles 34 to 37 establish the court system and vest judicial power exclusively in the courts. We examine what counts as the administration of justice, the constitutional prohibition on non-judicial bodies exercising judicial power, and the landmark cases applying this principle including McDonald v Bord na gCon.

title: The President - Articles 12 to 14
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The President occupies a unique constitutional position head of state, but largely ceremonial. This episode covers the powers and functions of the President under Articles 12 to 14, including the absolute discretions and the limited but significant power to refer Bills to the Supreme Court under Article 26.

title: Delegated Legislation and Henry VIII Clauses
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: When the Oireachtas empowers a minister to make statutory instruments, constitutional limits apply. This episode covers the principles governing delegated legislation in Ireland, the controversial Henry VIII clause, and the cases that have struck down overbroad grants of legislative power.

title: Grounds and Procedure for Judicial Review
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Judicial review is the mechanism by which courts supervise the exercise of public power. This episode covers the procedural requirements under Order 84 RSC, the distinction between certiorari, mandamus, and prohibition, and the grounds on which public decisions can be challenged in Ireland.

title: Constitutional Remedies
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: When a constitutional right is breached, what remedies are available? This episode examines the remedies courts can grant for constitutional violations declarations, injunctions, damages, and the writ of habeas corpus and the principles that govern how courts exercise their remedial discretion.

title: The Article 26 Reference Procedure
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 26 allows the President to refer a Bill to the Supreme Court to test its constitutionality before it is signed into law. This episode covers how the procedure works, its consequences (including the immunity of a bill that passes from future challenge), and the key Article 26 references in Irish constitutional history.

title: Equality Before the Law - Article 40.1
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 40.1 guarantees that all citizens shall be held equal before the law. This episode examines what that equality guarantee means in practice, how Irish courts distinguish between permissible classification and unconstitutional discrimination, and the leading cases shaping this right.

title: Unenumerated Personal Rights - Article 40.3
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 40.3 protects the personal rights of citizens including rights not explicitly named in the Constitution. This episode traces the development of unenumerated rights doctrine from Ryan v AG [1965] through to the modern case law, and examines which rights have been recognised and which have been rejected.

title: Liberty of the Person - Article 40.4
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 40.4 protects the right to personal liberty and provides for habeas corpus. We examine the constitutional guarantee against unlawful detention, the procedure for challenging detention, and the Irish cases that have shaped this fundamental right. A practical and exam-relevant topic.

title: Inviolability of the Dwelling - Article 40.5
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 40.5 guarantees that the dwelling of every citizen is inviolable. This episode examines the scope of this protection, how it interacts with search warrant powers, and the cases in which courts have found breaches of this constitutional guarantee. Relevant for both constitutional and criminal law questions.

title: Freedom of Expression - Article 40.6.1(i)
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Freedom of expression is protected by Article 40.6.1(i) but it is not absolute the Constitution itself permits restrictions for public order, morality, and the authority of the State. This episode maps out the constitutional framework for expression, the balancing approach of Irish courts, and the interaction with defamation and privacy law.

title: Freedom of Assembly and Association - Article 40.6.1(ii) and (iii)
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Articles 40.6.1(ii) and (iii) protect the rights to peaceful assembly and to form associations. We examine the scope of these rights, the constitutional grounds for restricting them, and the Irish cases that have tested the boundaries between lawful protest and criminal conduct.

title: The Family - Article 41
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 41 recognises the Family as the primary unit of society and provides it with constitutional protection. This episode examines the traditional and evolving definition of family under Irish constitutional law, the key cases on marital privacy, and the tension between Article 41 and the rights of individual family members.

title: Education - Article 42
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 42 places primary responsibility for a child's education on parents, not the State. This episode covers the constitutional right to education, the limits of state intervention, the obligation to provide for free primary education, and the leading cases on the parent-state relationship in education.

title: Children's Rights - Article 42A
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 42A, inserted by referendum in 2012, gave express constitutional recognition to children's rights. This episode examines what Article 42A adds to the existing constitutional framework, the best interests of the child standard, and how the provision interacts with parental rights under Article 41.

title: Right to Private Property - Article 43
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 43 guarantees the right to private property but expressly permits the State to regulate its exercise in the interests of the common good. This episode covers the scope of the property right, the test for proportionate regulation versus unconstitutional interference, and the key Irish property rights cases.

title: Freedom of Religion - Article 44
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 44 guarantees freedom of conscience and the practice of religion. We examine the separation of Church and State under the Irish Constitution, the prohibition on endowment of religion, and how courts have approached cases involving conflicts between religious freedom and other constitutional rights.

title: Directive Principles of Social Policy - Article 45
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 45 sets out principles of social policy to guide the Oireachtas but they are expressly stated to be non-justiciable. This episode examines what Article 45 contains, why it cannot be enforced in court, and how it nonetheless influences constitutional interpretation and legislative policy.

title: Due Course of Law - The Core Guarantee
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 38.1 provides that no person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law. This episode examines what that guarantee means, the constitutional requirements for a fair criminal trial, and how the due course of law standard sits alongside the specific rights in Articles 38 and 40.

title: Vague Offences
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The Constitution prohibits criminal liability being imposed on vague or uncertain offences. This episode examines the vagueness doctrine in Irish constitutional law, the test applied by courts, and the cases in which legislation has been struck down for failing to define a criminal offence with sufficient precision.

title: Presumption of Innocence and Reverse Onus
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Every accused person is presumed innocent until proven guilty. This episode examines the constitutional status of the presumption of innocence, when the Oireachtas can legitimately reverse the burden of proof, and the test Irish courts apply to assess whether a reverse onus clause is constitutionally permissible.

title: Undue Delay
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: A person accused of a criminal offence has a constitutional right to a trial with reasonable expedition. This episode covers the law on undue delay in Irish criminal proceedings when delay becomes unconstitutional, the test from PM v Malone, and the remedy of prohibiting a trial on grounds of delay.

title: Duty to Seek and Preserve Evidence
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The State has a duty to seek out and preserve evidence that may be relevant to an accused's defence. This episode examines that constitutional obligation, the consequences of failure including the possibility of a stay on proceedings and the leading Irish cases on prosecutorial disclosure obligations.

title: Pre-Trial Publicity and the Fair Trial Guarantee
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Extensive media coverage of a criminal case can compromise a defendant's right to a fair trial. We examine when pre-trial publicity crosses a constitutional line, the test for granting a prohibition or change of venue, and how Irish courts balance freedom of expression with the right to a fair trial.

title: Fair Procedures and Natural Justice
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The constitutional right to fair procedures audi alteram partem and nemo iudex in causa sua applies wherever a decision affects a person's rights. This episode covers the natural justice principles, their constitutional basis, and the case law on what fair procedures actually require in practice.

title: The Special Criminal Court - Article 38.3
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Article 38.3 permits the establishment of special courts for the trial of offences where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice. This episode examines the constitutional basis for the Special Criminal Court, the jury trial guarantee, and the case law on when referral to the SCC is constitutionally justified.

title: Right to Legal Representation and Legal Aid
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The right of access to a lawyer is a fundamental aspect of the right to a fair trial. This episode covers the constitutional right to legal representation in criminal proceedings, the State's obligation to provide legal aid where a person cannot afford a lawyer, and the key cases from Ireland and the ECtHR on this right.

title: International Law and Article 29 - Ireland's Dualist System
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Ireland operates a dualist system: international law does not automatically become part of Irish domestic law. Article 29 governs Ireland's relationship with the international legal order. This episode explains what that means, how treaties must be incorporated, and the consequences for Irish courts when interpreting rights with an international dimension.

title: Ireland and the EU - Article 29.4 and the Crotty Principle
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: Ireland's membership of the EU is constitutionally grounded in Article 29.4. This episode examines how EU law is accommodated in the Irish constitutional order, the Crotty principle requiring referendum approval for significant EU treaty changes, and the tension between EU primacy and Irish constitutional sovereignty.

title: Primacy of EU Law - The Irish Constitutional Position
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: EU law claims primacy over national law but how does that sit with the Irish Constitution? This episode examines the Irish approach to the supremacy question, the cases that have addressed direct conflicts between EU law and constitutional provisions, and what happens when the two systems cannot be reconciled.

title: The ECHR in Irish Law
subject: Constitutional Law
episodeNotes: The European Convention on Human Rights was incorporated into Irish law by the ECHR Act 2003. This episode explains how the Convention operates in Irish domestic law, the interpretive obligation on courts, the declaration of incompatibility remedy, and the distinction between Convention rights and constitutional rights.

title: Offer and Acceptance
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: The formation of a contract begins with offer and acceptance, and getting this right is the foundation of the entire subject. This episode works through the rules on offer, invitation to treat, acceptance, communication, and revocation with the key Irish and English case law that FE-1 candidates must know.

title: Consideration
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: No contract is binding without consideration or is it? This episode examines the rules on consideration, the requirement that it be sufficient but not adequate, part payment of debt, past consideration, and the performance of existing duties. A technically demanding topic that rewards clear thinking.

title: Promissory Estoppel
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Promissory estoppel allows a party to enforce a promise even without consideration in certain circumstances. We examine the doctrine from its origins in Central London Property Trust v High Trees, its development in Irish law, and the critical question of whether estoppel can be used as a sword or only a shield.

title: Intention to Create Legal Relations
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Not every agreement is a contract the parties must intend to be legally bound. This episode covers the presumptions that apply in domestic and commercial contexts, how those presumptions can be rebutted, and the Irish cases where courts have had to decide whether parties truly intended legal relations.

title: Capacity to Contract
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Who can legally enter into a binding contract? This episode covers the capacity rules for minors, persons of unsound mind, intoxicated persons, and companies, including the special rules that apply to contracts made by or with minors under Irish law.

title: Formal Requirements
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Most contracts can be made orally, but some must be in writing or evidenced in writing to be enforceable. This episode covers the Statute of Frauds, contracts that require written form under Irish law, and the consequences of non-compliance including part performance as an equitable exception.

title: Express Terms and Representations
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: What is actually in the contract? This episode distinguishes statements that are contractual terms from those that are mere representations, explains how courts determine what has been incorporated, and covers the parol evidence rule and its exceptions. A fundamental distinction for FE-1 problem questions.

title: Conditions, Warranties and Innominate Terms
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Not all contractual terms carry the same consequences for breach. This episode distinguishes conditions (breach entitles termination) from warranties (breach sounds only in damages) and innominate terms, and works through the Irish and English cases on how courts classify terms and choose the appropriate remedy.

title: Implied Terms and Interpretation
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Terms can be implied into contracts by statute, by custom, or by the courts. This episode covers the tests for implying terms in fact and in law, the business efficacy and officious bystander tests, the modern approach to contractual interpretation, and the Consumer Rights Act 2022 implied terms.

title: Exemption Clauses
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A party may try to exclude or limit liability through an exemption clause but courts apply strict rules to them. We cover incorporation by signature, notice, and course of dealing; the contra proferentem rule; and the statutory controls under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 and Sale of Goods legislation.

title: Certainty of Terms
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A contract must be sufficiently certain to be enforceable. This episode examines when vagueness or incompleteness will prevent a contract from being binding, the mechanisms courts use to save uncertain agreements, and the distinction between agreements to agree and enforceable contracts.

title: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Sale of Goods
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: The Consumer Rights Act 2022 fundamentally overhauled consumer protection law in Ireland. This episode covers the implied terms as to title, description, quality, and fitness for purpose in contracts for the sale of goods, and the remedies available to consumers when goods do not conform.

title: Consumer Rights Act 2022 - Digital Content and Services
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: The 2022 Act broke new ground by extending statutory protections to contracts for digital content and digital services. This episode covers the conformity requirements for digital products, the remedies available, and why this is an increasingly important area for FE-1 candidates to understand.

title: Unfair Contract Terms
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Consumer contracts must not contain unfair terms. This episode examines the unfairness test under the Consumer Rights Act 2022, the indicative list of potentially unfair terms, the consequences of a term being found unfair, and how these protections sit alongside the common law rules on exemption clauses.

title: Misrepresentation
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A misrepresentation is a false statement of fact made before the contract is concluded that induces the other party to enter. This episode covers the types of misrepresentation fraudulent, negligent, and innocent the available remedies including rescission and damages, and the Irish cases that illustrate the distinctions.

title: Mistake
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Mistake is one of the most complex vitiating factors in contract law. This episode examines common mistake, mutual mistake, and unilateral mistake, when mistake will void or voidable a contract at common law and in equity, and the leading cases from Ireland and England.

title: Duress
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A contract obtained through unlawful pressure duress may be voidable. This episode covers physical duress, economic duress, the development of the doctrine in Irish and English case law, and the distinction between legitimate commercial pressure and illegitimate coercion.

title: Undue Influence
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Where one party exploits a position of influence over another to procure a contract, the doctrine of undue influence may apply. We cover actual and presumed undue influence, the relationships that give rise to a presumption, the role of independent legal advice, and the landmark cases on third-party undue influence in banking transactions.

title: Discharge by Agreement and Performance
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A contract can come to an end in a number of ways. This episode covers discharge by performance including the rules on exact and substantial performance and discharge by agreement, examining when parties can validly agree to bring their obligations to an end.

title: Discharge by Breach and Repudiation
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: A sufficiently serious breach by one party can entitle the innocent party to terminate the contract. This episode covers the right to terminate for breach, anticipatory breach and repudiation, the election to affirm or accept repudiation, and the consequences of wrongful termination.

title: Discharge by Frustration
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: The doctrine of frustration applies when a supervening event makes performance of a contract radically different from what was agreed. We examine the test for frustration from Davis Contractors v Fareham, the types of events that can frustrate a contract, and the Irish case law including the consequences under the Civil Liability (Amendment) Act 1961.

title: Damages
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Damages are the primary remedy for breach of contract. This episode covers the expectation, reliance, and restitution measures of loss, the rules on remoteness from Hadley v Baxendale, the duty to mitigate, and the assessment of damages in Irish contract cases. A topic that rewards methodical exam technique.

title: Equitable Remedies in Contract
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Specific performance and injunction are the main equitable remedies for breach of contract. This episode examines when specific performance will be granted, the bars to the remedy, and how injunctions are used to enforce negative contractual obligations with reference to Irish case law throughout.

title: Privity of Contract
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: The doctrine of privity provides that only parties to a contract can enforce it or be bound by it. This episode examines the doctrine, the hardship it can cause, the common law and equitable exceptions, and the statutory modifications introduced in Ireland by the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act.

title: Illegal and Void Contracts
subject: Contract Law
episodeNotes: Some contracts are unenforceable because they are contrary to law or public policy. This episode covers contracts illegal at formation and contracts that become illegal in performance, contracts void for illegality, contracts in restraint of trade, and how courts apportion consequences between the parties.

title: Duress and Self-Defence
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Where a person commits a crime under a threat of death or serious injury, the defence of duress may apply. This episode covers the elements of duress, its limitations (particularly in murder cases), and the law on self-defence under Section 18 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997.

title: Defence of the Dwelling
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Section 2 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 and the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 provide a specific statutory framework for using force to defend one's home. This episode examines the scope of the defence, the force that is permissible, and how Irish courts have interpreted the dwelling protection.

title: Defence of the Dwelling
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Section 2 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 and the Criminal Law (Defence and the Dwelling) Act 2011 provide a specific statutory framework for using force to defend one's home. This episode examines the scope of the defence, the force that is permissible, and how Irish courts have interpreted the dwelling protection.

title: DVA 2018, Coercive Control and the Coco Act
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The Domestic Violence Act 2018 and the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023 (the Coco Act) represent a major development in Irish criminal law. This episode covers the offence of coercive control, the civil orders available under the DVA 2018, and the interaction between civil and criminal remedies for domestic abuse.

title: Public Order Offences
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 governs public order in Ireland. This episode covers the main offences threatening, abusive, or insulting behaviour, riot, violent disorder, and affray their elements, and how Irish courts have balanced public order enforcement with constitutional rights to assembly and expression.

title: Offences Against Justice and State Offences
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: This episode covers a range of offences directed at the administration of justice and the security of the State including perjury, perverting the course of justice, contempt of court, and offences under the Offences Against the State Act 1939. Less glamorous but important for a complete FE-1 knowledge base.

title: Courts, Classification and Arrest
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The structure of the Irish criminal court system determines where a case is tried and what procedural rules apply. This episode covers the courts' jurisdiction, the classification of offences for trial purposes, the law on arrest and detention under the Criminal Justice Act 1984, and the constitutional limits on Garda powers.

title: Bail and the Right to Silence
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: This episode covers two constitutional rights with significant practical implications. We examine the right to bail, the grounds for refusal under the Bail Act 1997 and Article 40.4, and the controversial erosion of the right to silence particularly the adverse inference provisions introduced by the Criminal Justice Act 2007.

title: Characteristics and Indicia of a Crime
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: What makes an act a crime rather than a civil wrong? This episode examines the essential characteristics that distinguish criminal from civil liability, the role of the State in prosecution, and the general indicia courts use to classify an obligation as criminal in nature. The conceptual foundation for the entire subject.

title: Classification of Criminal Offences
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Irish criminal offences are classified in different ways indictable vs summary, arrestable vs non-arrestable. This episode covers the classification system, why it matters for procedure, trial venue, and sentencing, and the impact of the Criminal Law Act 1997 on arrest powers.

title: Actus Reus - Core Principles, Voluntariness and Omissions
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Every crime requires an act (actus reus) but what counts? This episode examines the voluntariness requirement, the general rule that omissions cannot ground criminal liability, and the narrow exceptions where a duty to act has been recognised. Precise knowledge of the actus reus is essential for problem questions.

title: Causation
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Where a crime requires a result, the prosecution must prove the accused caused it. This episode covers factual causation (the but-for test), legal causation, the year-and-a-day rule (now abolished), novus actus interveniens, and the Irish case law on causation in homicide cases.

title: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Mens rea is the mental element of a crime. This episode covers the full range specific intent, basic intent, subjective and objective recklessness, negligence, and strict liability with the Irish and English cases that have shaped the modern law. Getting mens rea right is the difference between a pass and a fail in most FE-1 criminal scenarios.

title: Mens Rea - Intention, Recklessness and Negligence
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Mens rea is the mental element of a crime. This episode covers the full range specific intent, basic intent, subjective and objective recklessness, negligence, and strict liability with the Irish and English cases that have shaped the modern law. Getting mens rea right is the difference between a pass and a fail in most FE-1 criminal scenarios.

title: Murder - Definition, Mens Rea and Capital Murder
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Murder is the most serious offence in the criminal calendar. This episode covers the common law and statutory definition of murder in Ireland, the mens rea of malice aforethought, the distinction between murder and capital murder under the Criminal Justice Act 1964, and how intention is established in practice.

title: Voluntary Manslaughter and Provocation
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: A killing that would otherwise be murder may be reduced to manslaughter if the accused was provoked. This episode examines the defence of provocation in Irish law, the objective and subjective elements, the standard applied, and how the Law Reform Commission has proposed reforming this area.

title: Involuntary Manslaughter and Dangerous Driving Causing Death
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Involuntary manslaughter covers unlawful killings where the accused did not intend to kill. We examine unlawful act manslaughter and gross negligence manslaughter, the dangerous driving offence under the Road Traffic Act, and how Irish courts have approached causation and recklessness in fatal accident cases.

title: Assisted Suicide and the Right to Die
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: This episode examines the law on assisted suicide and euthanasia in Ireland, the offence under Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993, and the constitutional challenges that have been brought before the Irish courts and the European Court of Human Rights on the right to die with dignity.

title: Assault, Assault Causing Harm and Serious Harm
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 restructured the law on assault in Ireland. This episode covers Section 2 (assault), Section 3 (assault causing harm), and Section 4 (causing serious harm), the mens rea requirements for each, and the hierarchy of offences that FE-1 candidates need to navigate in problem questions.

title: Harassment and Stalking - NFOATP 1997
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Section 10 and Section 10A of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 criminalise harassment and stalking respectively. This episode covers the elements of each offence, the conduct that can constitute harassment or stalking, and the role of the Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act 2020.

title: Rape - Definition, Consent and the Abolition of Honest Belief
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The law on rape in Ireland was significantly reformed by the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. This episode covers the definition of rape, the consent framework introduced by the 2017 Act, the abolition of the honest belief defence, and what these changes mean for prosecuting sexual violence in Ireland.

title: Sexual Assault and Rape Under Section 4
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Beyond rape, Irish law recognises several other serious sexual offences. This episode covers sexual assault under Section 2 of the Criminal Law (Rape) Amendment Act 1990, rape under Section 4, and the consent and mens rea requirements that apply across these offences.

title: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Irish law provides specific and enhanced protections for children and protected persons against sexual offences. This episode covers the relevant offences, the age of consent, strict liability provisions, and the obligations on professionals who become aware of such offences.

title: Theft and Deception
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: The Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 modernised Irish property crime law. This episode covers the definition of theft, the requirement of dishonesty, the deception offences, and the case law on what constitutes appropriation and intention to permanently deprive. Property offences are a staple of FE-1 criminal problem questions.

title: Burglary and Aggravated Burglary
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Burglary involves entering a building as a trespasser with intent to commit certain offences. This episode examines the elements of burglary and aggravated burglary under the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001, the meaning of 'building', and how courts have approached the intent requirement.

title: Handling Stolen Goods, Criminal Damage and Cybercrime
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: This episode covers three important property offences: handling stolen goods under the 2001 Act, criminal damage under the Criminal Damage Act 1991, and the emerging area of cybercrime under the Criminal Justice (Offences Relating to Information Systems) Act 2017.

title: Aid, Abet, Counsel and Procure - Secondary Liability
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Criminal liability is not limited to the person who physically commits the offence. This episode covers the four modes of secondary participation aiding, abetting, counselling, and procuring under Section 7 of the Criminal Law Act 1997, the mens rea required for each, and the Irish case law on accessories.

title: Common Design, Joint Enterprise and Withdrawal
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Where two or more people agree to commit a crime together, each may be liable for acts done in pursuance of that common design including acts they did not personally commit. This episode covers common design liability, the extent to which it can extend to unforeseen acts, and the conditions under which a participant can withdraw.

title: Conspiracy, Incitement and Attempt
subject: Criminal Law
episodeNotes: Incomplete crimes can still attract criminal liability. This episode covers the inchoate offences of conspiracy, incitement, and attempt the required acts and mental elements for each, the point at which liability begins, and the impossibility defence. These offences appear regularly in FE-1 problem scenarios.

title: Foundations of Equity - The Equitable Maxims
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Equity developed as a body of law administered by the Court of Chancery to mitigate the harshness of the common law. This episode introduces the historical development of equity, the fusion of common law and equity courts, and the maxims that underpin equitable doctrine the principles that run through every topic in this subject.

title: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Intention and Subject Matter
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: For an express trust to be valid, three certainties must be present. This episode examines the first two certainty of intention (the requirement that the settlor clearly intends to create a trust) and certainty of subject matter (the requirement that the trust property be identifiable) with the leading case law from Ireland and England.

title: The Three Certainties - Certainty of Objects
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: The third certainty requires that the beneficiaries of a trust be identifiable. This episode covers the different tests for certainty of objects that apply to fixed trusts and discretionary trusts, the consequences of failing the test, and the leading cases including McPhail v Doulton [1971] and Re Baden's Deed Trusts [1973].

title: Secret Trusts - Fully Secret and Half-Secret Trusts
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: A secret trust allows a testator to communicate the true purpose of a bequest to the legatee without it appearing on the face of the will. This episode examines the distinction between fully secret and half-secret trusts, the requirements for their validity, and the theoretical basis for their enforcement outside the Wills Act.

title: Mutual Wills
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Mutual wills arise where two testators agree to make wills in agreed terms and not to revoke them. This episode covers the doctrine of mutual wills, when the agreement becomes binding and irrevocable, how a constructive trust is imposed on the surviving testator's estate, and the Irish and English cases on this difficult area.

title: Trustees' Duties - Investment and the Prudent Trustee Standard
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Trustees must invest trust property prudently. This episode covers the duty of investment under the Trustee Act 1893 and the Charities Act 2009, the prudent trustee standard, the requirement for diversification, and the cases that have defined what counts as imprudent investment leading to personal liability.

title: Trustees' Duties - Duty Not to Profit and Self-Dealing
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: A trustee must not profit from their position or place themselves in a position of conflict. This episode covers the no-profit rule, the self-dealing rule, the fair dealing rule, and the cases including Keech v Sandford and Boardman v Phipps that illustrate how strictly equity enforces these obligations.

title: Trustees' Powers - Delegation, Advancement and Maintenance
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Trustees have both duties and powers. This episode covers the power to delegate, the conditions under which delegation is permitted and the trustee's liability for delegates, the statutory power of advancement under the Succession Act 1965, and the power of maintenance for infant beneficiaries.

title: Resulting Trusts - Presumed and Automatic
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: A resulting trust arises where property returns to the person who transferred it. This episode distinguishes between automatic resulting trusts (where a trust fails) and presumed resulting trusts (arising from voluntary transfer and purchase money contributions), the presumption of advancement, and how Irish courts have applied the resulting trust doctrine in family property disputes.

title: Specific Performance and Other Equitable Remedies
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Specific performance compels a party to perform their contractual obligations rather than simply pay damages. This episode covers when specific performance will be granted, the bars to the remedy, rectification, rescission, and account of profits the full toolkit of equitable remedies that FE-1 candidates need to know.

title: Constitution of Trusts - The Rule in Milroy v Lord
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: A trust must be properly constituted to be enforceable. The rule in Milroy v Lord [1862] states that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. This episode examines what is required to constitute a trust, the different modes of transfer for different types of property, and the consequences of failure to constitute.

title: Constitution of Trusts - Exceptions and Equity Will Perfect
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: The general rule that equity will not perfect an imperfect gift has important exceptions. This episode covers the rule in Strong v Bird, donatio mortis causa, proprietary estoppel as a constituting mechanism, and the modern cases that have stretched the boundaries of what equity will treat as constituted.

title: Constructive Trusts - Categories and Common Intention
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: A constructive trust is imposed by law regardless of the parties' intentions. This episode covers the main categories of constructive trust in Irish and English law, with particular focus on the common intention constructive trust as it applies to shared homes a topic of enormous practical importance and regular FE-1 examination.

title: Constructive Trusts and Proprietary Estoppel
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Proprietary estoppel arises where a person relies on an assurance about property to their detriment. This episode examines the elements of proprietary estoppel, how it relates to and differs from the constructive trust, the remedies available, and the landmark Irish and English cases in this fast-developing area.

title: Third-Party Liability - Knowing Receipt and Dishonest Assistance
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: Where a third party receives trust property or assists in a breach of trust, they may be personally liable in equity. This episode covers the two heads of third-party liability knowing receipt and dishonest assistance the mental element required for each, and the House of Lords decision in Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan that reshaped the law.

title: Injunctions - Types and the Campus Oil Test
subject: Equity
episodeNotes: The injunction is equity's most powerful remedy. This episode covers prohibitory and mandatory injunctions, the interlocutory injunction and the Campus Oil test as applied in Ireland, the Mareva injunction, and the Anton Piller order. A practical topic with clear exam application.

title: Module Bonus Lesson 1
subject: Equity

title: Free Movement of Workers - Article 45 TFEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 45 TFEU guarantees free movement of workers within the EU. This episode covers the definition of 'worker', the rights conferred including equal treatment, access to employment, social and tax advantages and the Regulation 492/2011 framework that operationalises the right.

title: Workers - Family Members, Jobseekers and Rights After Employment
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The right to free movement extends beyond the worker themselves. This episode covers the rights of family members (including non-EU nationals) under Directive 2004/38, the rights of jobseekers, and what happens to EU law rights when a worker stops working including rights of permanent residence.

title: Derogations from Free Movement of Workers
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Free movement of workers is not absolute Member States may restrict it on grounds of public policy, public security, and public health. This episode examines the derogations under Directive 2004/38, the requirement of proportionality, the procedural safeguards for those facing expulsion, and the case law from the CJEU.

title: EU Citizenship - Origins, Nature and the Maastricht Innovation
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: EU citizenship was introduced by the Maastricht Treaty and gives every national of an EU Member State a direct relationship with the European Union. This episode covers the origins of citizenship, its relationship to national citizenship, and the foundational case law including Grzelczyk and Baumbast that has defined its content.

title: Right of Residence Under Directive 2004/38 - Conditions and the Welfare Dimension
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Directive 2004/38 consolidates and codifies EU citizenship and residence rights. This episode covers the three-tier structure of residence rights up to three months, three months to five years, and permanent residence the conditions that apply at each stage, and the Court's approach to welfare tourism claims.

title: Third Country National Family Members - Zambrano, McCarthy and Dereci
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The rights of non-EU family members of EU citizens can be highly complex. This episode examines the cases Zambrano, McCarthy, and Dereci that have tested the boundaries of EU citizenship rights, the principle that EU law must not deprive a citizen of the genuine enjoyment of their citizenship, and what these cases mean for Irish immigration law.

title: Political Rights, Consular Protection and the Constitutional Significance of EU Citizenship
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: EU citizenship comes with political rights including the right to vote and stand in European Parliament and municipal elections in any Member State. This episode also covers consular protection for EU citizens abroad, and reflects on the constitutional significance of a supranational citizenship that supplements but does not replace national citizenship.

title: The Equal Pay Principle - Article 157 TFEU and Defrenne
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 157 TFEU requires equal pay for equal work between men and women. This episode examines the history of the equal pay principle, the landmark Defrenne v Sabena trilogy, the concept of 'pay' under EU law, and the relationship between Article 157 and the Equal Pay Directive.

title: Equal Treatment Directives and the Broadened Equality Framework
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The EU's equality framework extends well beyond pay to cover access to employment, working conditions, and social security. This episode covers the Equal Treatment Directive, the Recast Directive 2006/54, and the expansion of EU equality law to grounds beyond sex including race, religion, disability, and age.

title: Freedom of Establishment - Article 49 TFEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 49 TFEU prohibits restrictions on the freedom of EU nationals to establish themselves in business in another Member State. This episode covers the scope of the establishment right, the distinction between establishment and services, the leading cases, and the permitted grounds for restricting establishment.

title: Free Movement of Services - Article 56 TFEU and the Services Directive
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 56 TFEU prohibits restrictions on the freedom to provide services across borders. This episode examines the scope of the right, its relationship with establishment, the Services Directive 2006/123 and the country of origin principle, and the cases that have tested what restrictions on services are compatible with EU law.

title: Origins of European Integration - Post-War to the EEC
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The European Union did not emerge fully formed. This episode traces the origins of European integration from the post-war political context through the establishment of the ECSC, EEC, and Euratom in the 1950s. Understanding why the EU was created is essential context for understanding how it operates and why it is constitutionally unique.

title: From the Single European Act to the Lisbon Treaty
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The EU's constitutional framework has evolved through a series of treaty revisions. This episode covers the key developments from the Single European Act 1986 through Maastricht, Amsterdam, Nice, and the Lisbon Treaty 2007, focusing on the changes most relevant to FE-1 candidates competence, institutions, and the Charter.

title: Brexit and Article 50 TEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The United Kingdom's withdrawal from the European Union was the first use of Article 50 TEU the withdrawal mechanism. This episode examines the Article 50 procedure, the legal and constitutional issues it raised, the Withdrawal Agreement, and the ongoing relevance of Brexit for Irish law including the Protocol/Windsor Framework.

title: The European Parliament - Composition, Powers and Democratic Role
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The European Parliament is the EU's directly elected institution. This episode covers its composition, legislative powers (co-decision/ordinary legislative procedure), budgetary role, scrutiny functions, and the ongoing debate about its role in addressing the EU's democratic deficit.

title: The Council and European Council - Intergovernmental Power
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Council of the EU represents the governments of the Member States and is the EU's primary legislative chamber. This episode distinguishes the Council from the European Council, covers voting arrangements (unanimity, QMV), and examines the intergovernmental dimension of EU decision-making.

title: The European Commission - Guardian of the Treaties
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Commission is the engine of European integration it proposes legislation, enforces EU law, and manages EU programmes. This episode covers the Commission's composition, appointment, legislative initiative monopoly, enforcement role under Article 258 TFEU, and its relationship with the other institutions.

title: The Court of Justice - Structure, Jurisdiction and Constitutional Role
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Court of Justice of the European Union (CJEU) is the supreme interpreter of EU law. This episode covers the structure of the CJEU and General Court, their respective jurisdictions, the Court's constitutional role in developing EU law through landmark judgments, and why understanding CJEU jurisprudence is central to the FE-1.

title: Democratic Deficit and Institutional Accountability
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The EU has long been criticised for its democratic deficit the perception that EU decision-making lacks sufficient democratic legitimacy. This episode examines the arguments for and against this critique, the institutional reforms introduced by the Lisbon Treaty, and what democratic accountability looks like in a supranational organisation.

title: Primary Law - Treaties and the Charter
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: EU primary law consists of the Treaties and the Charter of Fundamental Rights. This episode covers the TEU and TFEU, the hierarchy of EU norms, the legal status of the Charter following Lisbon, and how primary law constrains all other EU action. The constitutional bedrock of the entire subject.

title: Secondary Law - Regulations, Directives and Decisions
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Most EU law takes effect through secondary legislation under Article 288 TFEU. This episode distinguishes regulations (directly applicable), directives (requiring national implementation), and decisions (binding on addressees), and explains why the distinction matters enormously for questions of direct effect and Member State liability.

title: Delegated Acts, Implementing Acts and Comitology
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Commission can be empowered to adopt delegated and implementing acts under Articles 290 and 291 TFEU. This episode covers the distinction between these instruments, the comitology system for supervising Commission implementing powers, and the constitutional significance of the Lisbon Treaty's reform of this area.

title: General Principles of EU Law
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The CJEU has developed a body of general principles including proportionality, legal certainty, legitimate expectations, and fundamental rights that bind EU institutions and Member States when implementing EU law. This episode covers the main principles, their origins, and their application in FE-1 relevant case law.

title: Fundamental Rights - Development and the Charter
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Fundamental rights protection began in the EU as judge-made law before being codified in the Charter. This episode traces that development from Stauder and Internationale Handelsgesellschaft through to the Lisbon Charter and examines the scope of the Charter as a binding legal instrument.

title: Scope of the Charter - When Does It Apply to Member States?
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Charter binds Member States only when they are implementing EU law but when exactly does that occur? This episode examines Article 51 of the Charter, the CJEU's interpretation of its scope in cases like Fransson and Ispas, and the ongoing controversy about how broadly or narrowly the Charter's application to national measures should be read.

title: Supremacy of EU Law - Costa, Simmenthal and the Constitutional Dimension
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The supremacy of EU law is the fundamental constitutional principle of the EU legal order. This episode covers the foundational cases Costa v ENEL [1964] and Simmenthal [1978] the absolute nature of the supremacy claim, and how national courts (including Irish courts) have responded to it.

title: Direct Effect of Treaty Articles and Regulations - Van Gend and Defrenne
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Direct effect means that EU law provisions can create individual rights enforceable in national courts. This episode covers the criteria established in Van Gend en Loos [1963], the application to treaty articles and regulations, the distinction between vertical and horizontal direct effect, and the landmark Defrenne cases.

title: Direct Effect of Directives - Marshall, Van Duyn, Ratti and Emanation of the State
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Directives can have direct effect once the implementation deadline has passed. This episode covers the conditions for directive direct effect, the vertical/horizontal distinction, the emanation of the State doctrine from Marshall and Foster, and why the FE-1 regularly tests candidate ability to identify whether direct effect is available.

title: Indirect Effect - Von Colson, Marleasing and Limits in Criminal Cases
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Where direct effect is unavailable, indirect effect may provide a remedy. The duty of consistent interpretation from Von Colson and Marleasing requires national courts to interpret domestic law in light of EU directives. This episode covers the principle, its limits, and the prohibition on indirect effect being used against individuals in criminal proceedings.

title: The Francovich Principle - State Liability for Failure to Implement EU Law
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: If a Member State fails to implement a directive and an individual suffers loss as a result, the State may be liable in damages. This episode examines Francovich [1991], the three conditions for State liability, and how this doctrine effectively extended EU law enforcement to situations where direct effect was unavailable.

title: Brasserie du Pecheur, Factortame and the Sufficiently Serious Breach
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Francovich principle was extended in Brasserie du Pêcheur and Factortame III to cover all forms of Member State breach of EU law. This episode examines the sufficiently serious breach test, the factors courts consider, and the cases that have shaped how Francovich damages are assessed in practice.

title: National Procedural Autonomy and its Limits - Equivalence and Effectiveness
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: EU law claims but does not always impose procedural rules. The principle of national procedural autonomy allows Member States to set their own rules for enforcement subject to the equivalence and effectiveness requirements. This episode examines what those requirements demand and the cases in which national procedural rules have been found to breach EU law.

title: Judicial Review of EU Acts - Article 263 TFEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 263 TFEU allows the CJEU to review the legality of EU acts. This episode covers the reviewable acts, the four grounds of review lack of competence, infringement of an essential procedural requirement, infringement of the Treaties, and misuse of powers and the time limit for bringing an action.

title: Standing Under Article 263 - Privileged, Quasi-Privileged and Non-Privileged Applicants
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Not everyone can bring an annulment action. This episode examines the three categories of applicant under Article 263 TFEU, the restrictive standing requirements for private individuals following Plaumann, the reforms of the Lisbon Treaty, and whether the current standing rules adequately protect individuals from unlawful EU action.

title: The Plea of Illegality - Article 277 TFEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Where a regulation cannot be challenged directly under Article 263, a party may nonetheless challenge its legality indirectly under Article 277 TFEU. This episode covers when the plea of illegality is available, its relationship with Article 263, and the scope of its application to different categories of EU act.

title: Article 265 TFEU - Action for Failure to Act
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 265 TFEU provides a remedy where an EU institution unlawfully fails to act. This episode covers the conditions for bringing an action including the requirement to first call on the institution to act the standing rules, and the relationship between Article 265 and Article 263 actions.

title: The Preliminary Reference Mechanism - Article 267 TFEU and Foto-Frost
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 267 TFEU allows (and sometimes requires) national courts to refer questions of EU law to the CJEU. This episode covers the mechanism, the Foto-Frost principle (national courts cannot declare EU acts invalid), and why the preliminary reference procedure is the main way EU law reaches national courts and FE-1 exams.

title: The Mandatory Duty to Refer and Acte Clair - CILFIT
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Courts of last resort must refer questions of EU law unless the answer is already clear. This episode examines the CILFIT criteria for acte clair, the conditions under which a court can decline to refer, and the debate about whether national courts apply these conditions too liberally.

title: Effect of Preliminary Rulings and Temporal Limitations
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: A ruling of the CJEU on EU law has retroactive effect it declares what the law has always been. This episode covers the general and retroactive effect of preliminary rulings, the exceptional power of the Court to limit the temporal effects of a ruling, and the Irish cases that have raised questions about preliminary references.

title: The Customs Union - Articles 28 to 30 TFEU
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: The Customs Union lies at the heart of the EU single market. This episode examines the prohibition on customs duties and charges having equivalent effect under Articles 28 to 30 TFEU, the meaning of charges having equivalent effect, and the limited exceptions that justify inspection fees.

title: Article 110 TFEU - Internal Taxation and Discrimination
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Article 110 TFEU prohibits Member States from using internal taxation to discriminate against imported goods. This episode covers the two limbs of Article 110 direct discrimination and indirect protection the assessment of 'similar' and 'competing' goods, and the case law including Humblot and Commission v France.

title: Quantitative Restrictions and MEQRs - Dassonville, Cassis and Keck
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Articles 34 and 35 TFEU prohibit quantitative restrictions and measures having equivalent effect on free movement of goods. This episode covers the Dassonville formula, the Cassis de Dijon principle of mutual recognition, the Keck departure for selling arrangements, and how courts identify a measure having equivalent effect.

title: Article 36 TFEU - Justifications for Restricting Free Movement of Goods
subject: EU Law
episodeNotes: Even restrictions caught by Articles 34 and 35 may be justified. Article 36 TFEU provides an exhaustive list of grounds. This episode covers the permitted justifications public morality, public policy, public security, health, and protection of industrial property the proportionality requirement, and the mandatory requirements doctrine from Cassis.

title: Testamentary Capacity and Formal Validity of Wills
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A will must satisfy strict requirements to be valid. This episode examines the mental capacity requirements from Banks v Goodfellow, the formal requirements for execution under the Succession Act 1965 including the signature and witness requirements and the consequences of failure to comply with the formalities.

title: Revocation, Codicils and Alteration of Wills
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A valid will can be revoked or altered. This episode covers the methods of revocation a later will or codicil, a written declaration, and destruction with the intention to revoke the effect of marriage and divorce on an existing will, and the legal status of alterations made to a will after execution.

title: Construction of Wills
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: When the meaning of a will is unclear, courts must construe it.
