This episode examines the principles of testamentary construction including the ordinary meaning rule, the context rule, and the use of extrinsic evidence and the cases where courts have had to resolve ambiguities in how estates are distributed.

title: The Legal Right Share - Section 111
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A surviving spouse or civil partner has an absolute right to a share in the deceased's estate regardless of the will. Section 111 of the Succession Act 1965 sets out the legal right share. This episode covers when it arises, how it is calculated, and the priority it takes over other claims on the estate.

title: Provision for Children - Section 117
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Section 117 of the Succession Act 1965 allows a child to apply to court for provision out of a deceased parent's estate where the parent has failed in their moral duty. This episode examines the test, the factors courts consider, the limits on the remedy, and the key Irish cases on what constitutes a failure of moral duty.

title: Intestacy
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: When a person dies without a valid will, their estate is distributed according to the intestacy rules in Part VI of the Succession Act 1965. This episode covers the full distribution scheme, the priority of the spouse and children, distribution to more remote relatives, and the position where no relatives can be found.

title: Relativity of Title
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Irish land law is based on a system of relative rather than absolute title. This episode explains what that means that ownership of land is a question of who has the better claim and examines how the system of relativity of title operates in practice, including its implications for adverse possession and conveyancing.

title: The Law of Finding
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Who owns property that has been found? This episode examines the rules on finding when a finder has a good title against everyone except the true owner, the exception for things found embedded in land, the obligations of finders, and the cases that have wrestled with ownership of found objects in unusual circumstances.

title: Joint Tenancy and Tenancy in Common
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Two or more people can own land together, but in fundamentally different ways. This episode examines joint tenancy (with its right of survivorship) and tenancy in common (with separate shares), the four unities required for a joint tenancy, and how to determine which type of co-ownership exists in a given case an essential skill for FE-1.

title: Creation of Co-Ownership Under the LCLRA 2009
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: The Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 changed the rules on creating co-ownership. This episode examines how the 2009 Act modified the pre-existing law, the default position where an instrument is ambiguous, and the practical implications for conveyancers advising clients on how to hold property.

title: Severance of Joint Tenancy
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A joint tenancy can be severed converting a joint tenancy into a tenancy in common in a number of ways. This episode examines the methods of severance (act, mutual agreement, course of dealing), the effect of severance, and the controversial question of whether unilateral notice can sever a joint tenancy under Irish law.

title: Rights and Obligations of Co-Owners
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Co-owners have both rights and obligations in respect of the co-owned property. This episode covers the right to occupy, the right to an account of profits, the obligation not to commit waste, the order for partition or sale under the LCLRA 2009, and the court's approach where co-owners cannot agree.

title: Adverse Possession - The Basic Framework
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A person who occupies another's land without permission for the limitation period can extinguish the paper owner's title. This episode introduces the framework for adverse possession in Ireland under the Statute of Limitations 1957, the rationale for the doctrine, and how it has been modified by the LCLRA 2009.

title: Requirements for Adverse Possession
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Adverse possession requires actual possession that is open, continuous, exclusive, and adverse. This episode examines each requirement in detail, the animus possidendi requirement, the meaning of adverse use, and the leading Irish cases on what conduct is and is not sufficient to establish possession.

title: Adverse Possession - Special Cases and Advanced Issues
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Adverse possession throws up complex scenarios in practice. This episode covers adverse possession against a future interest, the effect of acknowledgment, the special rules for State land, adverse possession of registered land under the Registration of Title Act 1964, and the human rights dimension following JA Pye v United Kingdom.

title: Nature and Requirements of Easements
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: An easement is a right to use another's land in some defined way. This episode covers the definition of an easement from Re Ellenborough Park [1956], the four requirements dominant and servient tenement, the right must accommodate the dominant tenement, the tenements must be separately owned, and the right must be capable of forming the subject matter of a grant.

title: Acquisition of Easements
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Easements can be acquired expressly, impliedly, or by prescription. This episode covers express grant and reservation, the rule in Wheeldon v Burrows, the rule under Section 6 of the Conveyancing Act 1881, implied necessity, and the various routes to acquiring an easement on the grant of part of a landowner's estate.

title: Prescription
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Prescription allows an easement to be acquired through long use. This episode examines the three methods of prescription available in Ireland common law prescription, lost modern grant, and the Prescription Act 1832 their requirements, their differences, and how the LCLRA 2009 reformed this area.

title: Extinguishment of Easements and Profits a Prendre
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Easements and profits can be brought to an end in a number of ways. This episode covers extinguishment by express release, implied release (abandonment), unity of ownership, and statutory extinguishment, and distinguishes the rules for easements from those applicable to profits à prendre.

title: Freehold Covenants at Common Law
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A covenant is a contractual promise about the use of land. At common law, the benefit of a covenant can run to successors but the burden traditionally cannot. This episode examines the rule in Tulk v Moxhay, the conditions for the transmission of benefit and burden at common law, and why the common law rules are largely insufficient on their own.

title: Freehold Covenants in Equity and the LCLRA 2009
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Equity fills the gap left by the common law by allowing the burden of restrictive covenants to run with the land under the rule in Tulk v Moxhay. This episode covers the conditions for the burden to run in equity, the conditions for the benefit to pass, the LCLRA 2009 reforms, and the discharge and modification of freehold covenants.

title: The Family Home Protection Act 1976
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 provides that a spouse cannot convey the family home without the prior written consent of the other spouse. This episode examines the scope and application of this protection, what constitutes the family home, the meaning of prior written consent, and the consequences of non-compliance.

title: Civil Partners and Cohabitants
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 extended similar protections to civil partners and created a framework for property claims by qualifying cohabitants. This episode covers the shared home protection for civil partners and the redress scheme for cohabitants on relationship breakdown or death.

title: Family Law Acts and Property Adjustment Orders
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: On the breakdown of a marriage or civil partnership, courts have extensive powers to adjust property rights. This episode covers the property adjustment order jurisdiction under the Family Law Act 1995 and the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996, the factors courts consider, and the interaction with the general law of property.

title: Bankruptcy and the Family Home
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: When one co-owner is declared bankrupt, the family home may be at risk. This episode examines the rights of a trustee in bankruptcy in respect of the family home, the balancing exercise courts must perform between the interests of creditors and the needs of the family, and the relevant provisions of the Bankruptcy Act 1988.

title: Historical Methods of Creating Mortgages
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Mortgages have a complex legal history. This episode traces the development of mortgage law from the common law conveyance with a proviso for redemption, through the equitable mortgage, to the modern statutory charge under the LCLRA 2009. Understanding the history is key to understanding the modern law.

title: Mortgagee's Remedies on Default
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: When a mortgagor defaults, the mortgagee has a range of remedies. This episode covers the power of sale, the right to take possession, foreclosure (now effectively abolished), the appointment of a receiver, and the procedural requirements under Irish law including the requirement for a possession order before a bank can evict.

title: The Equity of Redemption and Clogs on the Equity
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Equity protects mortgagors through the equity of redemption the right to redeem the mortgage even after the legal date for redemption has passed. This episode examines the equity of redemption, the doctrine against clogs on the equity, the unconscionability cases, and what terms in a mortgage will be struck down.

title: Consumer Mortgage Protections
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Irish law provides significant protections for residential mortgage borrowers in financial difficulty. This episode covers the Central Bank's Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears, the Abhaile scheme, the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 amendments, and the court's jurisdiction to adjourn possession proceedings where a mortgagor has made genuine efforts to resolve arrears.

title: Types of Licences
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A licence is a permission to be on land that does not create a proprietary interest. This episode introduces the different categories of licence bare licence, contractual licence, and licence coupled with an interest and explains why the distinction between a licence and a property right matters so much in practice.

title: Contractual Licences
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A contractual licence is one that arises from a contract between the parties. This episode examines when a contractual licence is revocable, the injunction remedy to prevent wrongful revocation, and the controversial question of whether a contractual licence can bind third parties an area where Irish and English law have diverged.

title: Proprietary Estoppel in Land Law
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Proprietary estoppel can give rise to a right in land where a person has relied on a landowner's assurance to their detriment. This episode covers the three elements assurance, reliance, and detriment the Irish cases from McMahon v Kerry County Council onwards, and the discretionary nature of the remedies courts award.

title: Rights of Residence
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A right of residence is a personal right to occupy land and is commonly created in family settlements in Ireland. This episode examines the legal nature of a right of residence, whether it can bind successors, the tax implications, and the Irish case law on the scope and enforceability of residential rights.

title: Nature of a Lease and Distinction from Licence
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: The distinction between a lease and a licence is one of the most important and frequently examined questions in Irish property law. This episode examines the essential elements of a lease under the Street v Mountford [1985] test, the Irish approach, and how courts look to substance rather than label in determining which relationship exists.

title: Covenants in Leases
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: A lease contains obligations (covenants) on both landlord and tenant. This episode covers the implied and express covenants, the landlord's obligation to allow quiet enjoyment, the tenant's obligation to pay rent and repair, and the rules on when covenants in leases run with the land to bind successors in title.

title: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: The Residential Tenancies Act 2004 (as amended) fundamentally reformed the law governing private residential tenancies in Ireland. This episode covers the security of tenure provisions, the four-year Part 4 tenancy, the grounds for termination, the rent review process, and the role of the Residential Tenancies Board in dispute resolution.

title: Commercial Leases and Ground Rents
subject: Property Law
episodeNotes: Commercial leases are governed largely by the common law of landlord and tenant, modified by specific legislation. This episode covers the Landlord and Tenant Acts, the rights of commercial tenants to new tenancies, the ground rents legislation, and the process by which ground rent tenants can acquire the freehold interest in their property.

title: Trespass to the Person - Battery
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Battery is the direct and intentional application of force to another person without consent. This episode covers the elements of battery act, directness, intention, and lack of consent the medical context where consent is particularly significant, and the Irish case law on what constitutes sufficient force.

title: Trespass to the Person - Assault and False Imprisonment
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Assault and false imprisonment are two distinct trespass torts that protect personal autonomy. This episode examines the elements of assault an intentional act causing reasonable apprehension of imminent battery and false imprisonment, the requirement of total restraint, and the cases on what constitutes a sufficiently complete restriction of movement.

title: Trespass - Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Irish tort law recognises a cause of action for the intentional or reckless infliction of emotional distress in certain circumstances. This episode examines the elements of this tort, its relationship to the intentional trespass torts, the threshold of seriousness required, and how it interacts with the remedies available under the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997.

title: Trespass to Land
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Trespass to land is direct, intentional, and unjustified interference with another's possession of land. This episode covers the elements of the tort including the extended reach of trespass above and below the surface the defences of licence and justification by law, and the remedies including injunction and mesne profits.

title: Private Nuisance
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Private nuisance protects a landowner's right to the reasonable use and enjoyment of their land. This episode covers the elements of private nuisance, the distinction from trespass, the factors relevant to reasonableness, the rule in Sturges v Bridgman, and the Irish cases on noise, smells, and other interferences with land enjoyment.

title: Public Nuisance and the Rule in Rylands v Fletcher
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Public nuisance and the rule in Rylands v Fletcher are related but distinct heads of tortious liability. This episode examines public nuisance as both a crime and a tort, the requirements for a private action for public nuisance, and the rule in Rylands v Fletcher whether it survives as a separate tort in Ireland following the 2009 LCLRA reforms.

title: Strict Liability - Liability for Animals
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Irish law imposes strict liability in certain circumstances for damage caused by animals. This episode covers the Animals Act (incorporating the common law scienter action), the strict liability for dangerous animals, and the rules on cattle trespass with attention to the modifications introduced by the Animals Act 1985 and relevant Irish case law.

title: Contributory Negligence and the Civil Liability Act 1961
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Where the claimant contributed to their own loss, damages may be reduced. This episode covers contributory negligence under the Civil Liability Act 1961, the apportionment of fault between claimant and defendant, the standard of care applied to claimants, and the Irish cases on how courts determine respective degrees of fault.

title: Volenti Non Fit Injuria and Limitation of Actions
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Volenti non fit injuria a complete defence where the claimant freely assumed the risk of harm and the limitation period are two important aspects of tort litigation. This episode covers the requirements for volenti, how courts distinguish it from contributory negligence, and the limitation periods applicable to different tort claims under the Statute of Limitations 1957.

title: Remedies in Tort - Damages
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Damages are the primary remedy in tort law. This episode covers the heads of general and special damages, the distinction between compensatory, aggravated, and exemplary damages, the assessment of non-pecuniary loss, the multiplier/multiplicand method for future loss, and the Book of Quantum for personal injury claims in Ireland.

title: Remedies in Tort - Injunctions
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: An injunction is an equitable remedy that can prevent the continuation of a tort. This episode covers the interlocutory injunction in the tort context, the Campus Oil test as applied in nuisance and trespass cases, mandatory injunctions, quia timet injunctions, and when Irish courts will award an injunction rather than damages.

title: Fatal Injuries and Survival of Actions - Civil Liability Act 1961
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The death of a victim raises special legal issues. This episode covers survival of actions under Part III of the Civil Liability Act 1961 (the estate's claim), the dependency claim under Part IV (the family's claim for loss of support), the assessment of fatal injury damages, and the interaction between both heads of claim.

title: Concurrent Wrongdoers - Civil Liability Act 1961
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Where two or more defendants contribute to the same loss, the rules on concurrent wrongdoing under Part III of the Civil Liability Act 1961 apply. This episode covers proportionate liability, the right of contribution between wrongdoers, and the claimant's ability to recover in full from any one concurrent wrongdoer.

title: Vicarious Liability - Employers and Independent Contractors
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: An employer can be held vicariously liable for the torts of their employees committed in the course of employment. This episode covers the employee/independent contractor distinction, the course of employment test, and the modern expansion of vicarious liability to non-employment relationships following Various Claimants v Catholic Child Welfare Society [2012].

title: Medical Negligence - The Dunne Principles
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Medical negligence is subject to special rules in Ireland derived from Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989]. This episode covers the Dunne principles as they apply to diagnosis, treatment, and the duty to warn patients of material risks, and examines how Irish courts have developed the law since Dunne in response to changing medical and social attitudes.

title: What is Tort Law?
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Tort law governs civil liability for wrongful acts that cause loss or harm to others. This episode introduces the conceptual foundations of tort law its purposes, its relationship to contract and criminal law, and the general structure of liability. The starting point for understanding everything that follows in this subject.

title: FE-1 Exam Strategy for Tort Law
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Knowing the law is one thing applying it effectively in an exam is another. This episode walks through the FE-1 exam approach for Tort Law, the typical question formats, how to structure a problem answer, which areas attract the most marks, and the most common mistakes candidates make in this subject.

title: Duty of Care - Historical Development and the Glencar Test
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The existence of a duty of care is the first element of negligence. This episode traces the development of duty from Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] through Anns, Caparo, and the Irish Glencar test from Glencar Explorations v Mayo County Council [2002]. Getting the duty analysis right is the foundation of every negligence answer.

title: Special Duty Situations - Omissions, Third Parties and Public Authorities
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The general duty of care does not extend to all situations. This episode examines the circumstances where duties are more restricted omissions, liability for acts of third parties, and the special treatment of public authorities with the Irish and English case law that draws the boundaries.

title: Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Recovery for pure economic loss is restricted in negligence. This episode covers the general exclusionary rule, the exception for negligent misstatement from Hedley Byrne [1964], the voluntary assumption of responsibility doctrine, and the Irish cases including Bank of Ireland v Smith that have shaped this complex area.

title: Breach of Duty - The Objective Standard and the Reasonable Person
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Breach of duty is assessed against the standard of the reasonable person. This episode examines who the reasonable person is, how courts adjust the standard for children, professionals, and those with special skills, and what the objective standard actually requires of defendants in Irish negligence cases.

title: Breach of Duty - Factors Courts Consider
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: In deciding whether a defendant breached their duty, courts weigh a number of factors. This episode covers the probability of harm, the gravity of potential injury, the cost of precautions, the social utility of the defendant's conduct, and how these factors are balanced in Irish negligence cases using the Dunne principles for professional negligence.

title: Factual Causation - The But-For Test
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The claimant must prove the defendant's breach caused their loss. This episode examines the but-for test, its application in straightforward cases, and the difficulties it creates in cases involving multiple potential causes including the Barker v Corus (now Sienkiewicz) material contribution exception.

title: Novus Actus Interveniens
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: A novus actus interveniens a new intervening act can break the chain of causation between the defendant's breach and the claimant's loss. This episode examines when an intervening act by a third party or the claimant themselves will break the chain, and the test of reasonable foreseeability that Irish courts apply.

title: Material Contribution and Complex Causation
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Some cases do not fit neatly into the but-for framework. This episode covers material contribution to injury, material contribution to risk, the Fairchild exception for mesothelioma cases, and how Irish courts have approached causation in cases where scientific uncertainty makes the but-for test difficult to apply.

title: Remoteness - The Wagon Mound Test and the Thin Skull Rule
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Even where causation is established, a defendant is only liable for harm that was reasonably foreseeable. This episode covers the Wagon Mound test, the type/kind distinction, the thin skull (egg-shell skull) rule, and how Irish courts have balanced these principles to determine which losses are too remote.

title: Psychiatric Injury - Primary Victims
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The law on recovery for psychiatric injury is more restrictive than for physical harm. This episode examines the primary victim category where the claimant was within the zone of physical danger the Page v Smith rule, its application in Irish law, and the requirement that the psychiatric harm be medically recognised.

title: Psychiatric Injury - Secondary Victims and Kelly v Hennessy
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Secondary victims suffer psychiatric injury through witnessing harm to others. This episode covers the control mechanisms from Alcock v Chief Constable and the Irish Supreme Court's adoption of a modified test in Kelly v Hennessy [1995] proximity of relationship, proximity to the event, and perception through unaided senses.

title: Occupiers' Liability - The Occupiers' Liability Act 1995
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The Occupiers' Liability Act 1995 replaced the common law categories of visitor, licensee, and trespasser with a statutory framework. This episode covers the three categories of entrant visitor, recreational user, and trespasser the duty owed to each, and the role of warning notices and exclusion clauses under the Act.

title: Product Liability - Common Law and the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Manufacturers, suppliers, and importers can face liability when their products cause harm. This episode covers the common law position from Donoghue v Stevenson, the strict liability regime under the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 (implementing the EU Product Liability Directive), and the defences available to producers.

title: Defamation - The Defamation Act 2009 and the Elements of the Tort
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The Defamation Act 2009 overhauled Irish defamation law. This episode covers the elements of the tort a defamatory statement about the plaintiff, publication to a third party, and identification the abolition of the distinction between libel and slander, and the single publication rule introduced by the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2024.

title: Defamation - Defences Under the 2009 Act
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: The 2009 Act provides a range of defences to a defamation claim. This episode covers truth, absolute and qualified privilege, honest opinion, innocent publication, and the fair and reasonable publication defence. Knowing which defence applies in a given scenario is a core FE-1 exam skill.

title: Defamation - Remedies and Practical Exam Application
subject: Tort Law
episodeNotes: Remedies in defamation include damages, correction orders, and declaratory orders under the 2009 Act. This episode covers the range of remedies, the factors courts consider in assessing damages, the prohibition on juries and the shift to judge-alone trials under the 2024 Act, and how to structure a defamation answer in the FE-1 exam.
